`blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_TensorObjSubstrate.tex` — ONLY this chapter.

## Goal
Add a new subsection blueprinting the **pullback-monoidality / invertibility-under-pullback substrate**
for general scheme morphisms. This is the prerequisite for re-basing the relative-Picard consumer
(`OnProduct`/RPF) onto the `IsInvertible` carrier (Route Y of A.1.c). Place it AFTER the existing
restriction-compatibility material (`lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`) and BEFORE / separate from the
group-law section `sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier` (do NOT modify the group-law section).

## Strategy context (the slice that matters)
The project's Picard group `picCommGroup` (DONE) is carried on tensor-invertibility
`IsInvertible M := ∃ N, Nonempty (M ⊗_X N ≅ 𝒪_X)` (Stacks 0B8K). The relative-Picard consumer needs to
re-base its line-bundle carrier `OnProduct` onto `IsInvertible`, and its functorial pullback maps
(`pullbackAlongProjection` along the projection `C×_S T → T`, `functorial` along the base-change
`g_C : C×_S T' → C×_S T`) require that **pullback preserves tensor-invertibility** for GENERAL scheme
morphisms (these are not open immersions, not flat).

IMPORTANT distinction to record in the prose: the existing `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso` handles only the
OPEN-IMMERSION case, and it does so by routing through `pushforward`/`restrictScalars` (the LAX right
adjoint, strong-monoidal there only because an open immersion's ring map is a local isomorphism). The
general case must instead use the LEFT adjoint — pullback = inverse image = extension of scalars — which
is **strong monoidal for ANY ring map** (no iso hypothesis). This is the whole reason the general lemma
is a separate result, not a corollary of the open-immersion one.

## Required new blocks (give each a `\lean{...}` pin and a precise mathematical proof sketch)

1. **`lem:pullback_tensor_iso`** — pin `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackTensorIso}`.
   Statement: for any morphism of schemes `f : Y ⟶ X` and `M, N ∈ Scheme.Modules X`,
   `(Scheme.Modules.pullback f).obj (tensorObj M N) ≅ tensorObj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback f).obj M)
   ((Scheme.Modules.pullback f).obj N)`, natural in `M, N`.
   `\uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}`.
   % SOURCE: Stacks tag for `lemma-tensor-product-pullback`, references/stacks-modules.tex lines 2392–2400.
   % SOURCE QUOTE: copy verbatim L2392–2400 (the statement `f^*(F⊗G) = f^*F ⊗ f^*G functorially`).
   Proof sketch (mirror the structure of `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso` but via the LEFT adjoint):
   - `tensorObj M N` is by definition the sheafification of the presheaf tensor
     `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj M.val N.val`.
   - The genuine Mathlib lemma `SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback` moves the pullback INSIDE the
     sheafification (it is general — holds for any ring-sheaf map, already used for the open-immersion
     case), reducing the goal to the presheaf level:
     `(PresheafOfModules.pullback φ).obj (M.val ⊗ₚ N.val) ≅ (pullback M).val ⊗ₚ (pullback N).val`
     where `φ = f`'s ring-sheaf map and `⊗ₚ = PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj`.
   - The presheaf-level pullback `PresheafOfModules.pullback φ` is STRONG monoidal: sectionwise it is
     extension of scalars, and Mathlib provides `(extendScalars f).Monoidal` (strong-monoidal extension
     of scalars for any ring hom, in `Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Monoidal/Adjunction.lean`).
     The strong-monoidal tensorator `μ` of this functor furnishes the presheaf comparison; sheafifying
     it gives the claim.

2. **`lem:pullback_unit_iso`** — pin `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackUnitIso}`.
   Statement: for any `f : Y ⟶ X`, `(Scheme.Modules.pullback f).obj (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf)
   ≅ SheafOfModules.unit Y.ringCatSheaf` (i.e. `f^*𝒪_X ≅ 𝒪_Y`).
   Proof sketch: the monoidal unit is preserved by the strong-monoidal pullback — the unit comparison
   `ε` of `(extendScalars f).Monoidal` is the iso `S ⊗_R R ≅ S`; at the sheaf level this descends to
   `f^*𝒪_X ≅ 𝒪_Y`. Note: a Mathlib morphism `SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit` exists (it is used in
   `IsLocallyTrivial.pullback`, where it is an iso for open immersions via finality); flag whether it is
   an iso for general `f` or whether the iso must come from the `extendScalars` unit comparison instead.

3. **`lem:isinvertible_pullback`** — pin `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.IsInvertible.pullback}`.
   Statement: for any `f : Y ⟶ X` and `M ∈ Scheme.Modules X`, `IsInvertible M →
   IsInvertible ((Scheme.Modules.pullback f).obj M)`.
   `\uses{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible, lem:pullback_tensor_iso, lem:pullback_unit_iso}`.
   % SOURCE: Stacks `lemma-pullback-invertible`, references/stacks-modules.tex lines 4142–4147 (statement)
   % and lines 4149–4157 (proof).
   % SOURCE QUOTE: copy verbatim L4142–4147.
   % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: copy verbatim L4149–4157.
   Proof sketch (the Stacks proof, in project notation): from `IsInvertible M` get a witness `N` with
   `e : M ⊗_X N ≅ 𝒪_X`. The pullback witness is `(pullback f).obj N`; the required iso
   `(pullback f).obj M ⊗_Y (pullback f).obj N ≅ 𝒪_Y` is
   `(pullbackTensorIso).symm ≪≫ (pullback f).mapIso e ≪≫ pullbackUnitIso`.

7	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
8	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
9	
10	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
11	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
12	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
13	\[
14	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
15	  \qquad
16	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
17	\]
18	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
19	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
20	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
21	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
22	
23	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
24	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
25	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
26	% groups)
27	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
28	% SOURCE QUOTE:
29	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
30	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
31	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
32	% abelian groups defined by the formula
33	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
34	% \end{dfn}
35	%  [\dots]
36	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
37	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
39	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
40	% topologies  by
41	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
42	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
43	%  \end{dfn}"
44	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
45	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
46	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
47	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
48	homomorphisms.}
49	
50	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
51	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
52	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
53	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
54	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
55	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
56	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
57	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
58	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
59	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
60	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
61	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
62	structure. This is the content of
63	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
64	
65	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
66	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
67	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
68	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
69	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
70	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
71	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
72	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
73	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
74	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
75	\begin{enumerate}
76	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
77	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
78	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
79	    isomorphism classes;
80	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
81	    for \(\otimes\);
82	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
83	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
84	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
85	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
86	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
87	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
88	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
89	\end{enumerate}
90	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
91	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
92	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
93	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
94	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
95	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
96	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
97	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
98	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
99	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
100	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
101	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
102	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\); sheafification
103	of presheaves of modules is the localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the
104	locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\), so a full monoidal structure on
105	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) would follow once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) were
106	supplied.
107	
108	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not
109	pursued. The goal is the commutative \emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes
110	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so
111	it never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the
112	pentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
113	that the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the
114	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced
115	directly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
116	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
117	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\)
118	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise
119	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
120	assembles \emph{by hand} the commutative group of \(\otimes\)-invertible
121	\(\otimes\)-iso-classes --- the Picard group
122	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) --- each group-axiom field fed a single
123	existence-of-isomorphism. This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
124	fixed-ring \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\),
125	whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent
126	monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) that
127	Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring; the present construction cannot take that
128	\(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route, since the coherent monoidal category on
129	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is exactly what it
130	avoids. The
131	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
132	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse existentially.
133	The \emph{full} \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) / \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) coherent
134	monoidal category is therefore not built for the group law and is off the critical
135	path; what the group law does still need from this circle of ideas is the
136	\emph{existence} of the associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), realized as a
137	sheafification transport whose single open left-whisker obligation is closed by the
138	stalk--tensor commutation (d.2). The stalk apparatus (d.1/d.2) is accordingly
139	\emph{on} the critical path, while the coherent monoidal category itself is not.
140

293	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
294	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)

297	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate

300	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
301	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
302	
303	\begin{definition}
304	
305	
306	\leanok
307	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
308	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
309	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
310	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
311	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
312	  \[
313	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
314	  \]
315	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
316	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
317	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
318	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
319	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
320	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
321	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf

392	\begin{remark}
393	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
394	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
395	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
396	        lem:whisker_of_W}
397	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
398	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
399	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
400	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
401	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
402	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
403	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
404	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
405	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
406	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
407	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
408	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
409	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
410	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
411	
412	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
413	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
414	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
415	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
416	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
417	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
418	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
419	  fixed-base presheaves in
420	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
421	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
422	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
423	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). The \emph{full}
424	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance and the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal
425	  category are not on the critical path: the group law is realized without them. The
426	  single left-whisker existence lemma and its underlying d.1/d.2 stalk apparatus, by
427	  contrast, \emph{are} on the critical path, since the associator's sheafification
428	  transport consumes exactly that one whisker obligation
429	  (\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed via d.2).
430	
431	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
432	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
433	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
434	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
435	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
436	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
437	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
438	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
439	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
440	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
441	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
442	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
443	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
444	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
445	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
446	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
447	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
448	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
449	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
450	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
451	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
452	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
453	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
454	\end{remark}

456	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
457	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
458	
459	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
460	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
461	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
462	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
463	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
464	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
465	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
466	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
467	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
468	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
469	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
470	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
471	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
472	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
473	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
474	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
475	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
476	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
477	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
478	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
479	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
480	to the off-path route~(e).

\section{Pullback-monoidality: invertibility under a general scheme morphism}
\label{sec:tensorobj_pullback_monoidality}

The relative Picard consumer (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}) re-bases its
line-bundle carrier onto the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}, and its
functorial structure maps are \emph{pullback} maps of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules: the
projection pullback \(\pi_T^*\) along \(C \times_S T \to T\) and the base-change
pullback along \(C \times_S T' \to C \times_S T\). For these maps to descend to the
\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) carrier one needs that \emph{pullback preserves
\(\otimes\)-invertibility} for \emph{general} morphisms of schemes --- the
projection and the base-change morphism are neither open immersions nor flat.

This is a genuinely different fact from the open-immersion compatibility
\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, and not a corollary of it. That lemma handles
restriction along an open immersion, and it does so by routing through the
\emph{lax right} adjoint \(\mathtt{pushforward}/\mathtt{restrictScalars}\), which is
strong monoidal there \emph{only} because an open immersion's structure-sheaf ring
map is a local \emph{isomorphism} (\(f.\mathtt{appIso}\)). A general scheme
morphism has no such isomorphism, so the right-adjoint route is unavailable. The
general case instead uses the \emph{left} adjoint --- pullback \(=\) inverse image
\(=\) extension of scalars --- which is strong monoidal for \emph{any} ring map,
with no iso hypothesis: extension of scalars \(- \otimes_R S\) along \(R \to S\)
carries the tensorator \((M \otimes_R N) \otimes_R S \cong (M \otimes_R S)
\otimes_S (N \otimes_R S)\) unconditionally. This is the whole reason the general
lemma is a separate result.

\begin{lemma}
[Pullback commutes with $\otimes$ for a general scheme morphism]
  \label{lem:pullback_tensor_iso}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackTensorIso}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let
  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
  Writing \(f^* = \mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f\) for the inverse-image functor
  of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules, the canonical comparison
  \[
    f^*(M \otimes_X N)
      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
    (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N)
  \]
  is an isomorphism, natural in \(M\) and \(N\). No local-freeness, flatness, or
  open-immersion hypothesis is required: it holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
  and all morphisms \(f\).
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
  % \ref{lemma-tensor-product-pullback} (pullback of $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a
  % morphism of ringed spaces commutes with tensor product, functorially)
  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L2392-2400).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "\begin{lemma}
  % \label{lemma-tensor-product-pullback}
  % Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be
  % a morphism of ringed spaces. Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$
  % be $\mathcal{O}_Y$-modules. Then
  % $f^*(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_Y} \mathcal{G})
  % = f^*\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} f^*\mathcal{G}$
  % functorially in $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$.
  % \end{lemma}"
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
  \texttt{lemma-tensor-product-pullback}: for a morphism of ringed spaces
  \(f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)\) and \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules
  \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\), one has \(f^*(\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{G}) =
  f^*\mathcal{F} \otimes f^*\mathcal{G}\) functorially.}
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  The proof mirrors the structure of \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} but routes
  through the \emph{left} adjoint, where no isomorphism hypothesis on the ring map
  is needed.

  \emph{Step 1 (reduce to the presheaf level).} By
  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}, the substrate tensor \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
  sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
  \(M.\mathrm{val} \otimes^{p} N.\mathrm{val}\), where
  \(\otimes^{p} = \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\). The pullback
  commutes with sheafification: the genuine Mathlib lemma
  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\) --- which is general
  (it holds for any morphism of ring-sheaves, and is already consumed in the
  open-immersion case \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) --- supplies a natural
  isomorphism moving \(f^*\) inside the sheafification. The claim therefore reduces
  to the presheaf-level comparison
  \[
    (\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,(A \otimes^{p} B)
      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
    \bigl((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A\bigr)
      \;\otimes^{p}\;
    \bigl((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B\bigr),
  \]
  where \(\varphi\) is the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of rings
  induced by \(f\), and \(A = M.\mathrm{val}\), \(B = N.\mathrm{val}\).

  \emph{Step 2 (the presheaf pullback is strong monoidal).} The presheaf-level
  pullback \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\) is, sectionwise,
  \emph{extension of scalars} along the section ring maps of \(\varphi\) --- the
  left adjoint of \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\). Mathlib equips extension of scalars
  along \emph{any} ring homomorphism \(\psi\) with a strong-monoidal structure,
  \((\mathtt{extendScalars}\,\psi).\mathtt{Monoidal}\)
  (\texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Monoidal/Adjunction.lean}); its
  tensorator is the canonical isomorphism
  \((M \otimes_R N) \otimes_R S \cong (M \otimes_R S) \otimes_S (N \otimes_R S)\),
  valid with \emph{no} hypothesis on \(\psi\). Assembling these sectionwise
  tensorators against the presheaf restriction morphisms furnishes a strong-monoidal
  structure on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), whose monoidal
  comparison \(\mu\) is precisely the presheaf-level isomorphism required by Step~1.
  Sheafifying \(\mu\) and transporting along the
  \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\) isomorphism of Step~1 yields the asserted
  isomorphism \(f^*(M \otimes_X N) \cong (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N)\). Naturality in
  \(M, N\) is inherited from the naturality of \(\mu\) and of sheafification.

  The contrast with \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is exactly the adjoint used:
  there the comparison is produced from the \emph{right} adjoint
  \(\mathtt{pushforward}/\mathtt{restrictScalars}\), which is strong monoidal only
  because the open immersion makes the comparison ring map a (local) isomorphism;
  here the \emph{left} adjoint \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\) is strong monoidal for
  every ring map, so the hypothesis of an isomorphism is dropped entirely.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Pullback preserves the structure sheaf]
  \label{lem:pullback_unit_iso}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackUnitIso}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes. The pullback of the structure sheaf
  is the structure sheaf: there is a canonical isomorphism
  \[
    f^*\bigl(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)
      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
    \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,Y.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf},
    \qquad\text{i.e.}\qquad
    f^* \mathcal{O}_X \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_Y,
  \]
  where \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf} = \mathcal{O}_X\) is
  the monoidal unit of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  The monoidal unit is preserved by the strong-monoidal pullback of
  \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso}. At the section level the relevant comparison is the
  unit comparison \(\varepsilon\) of the strong-monoidal extension of scalars
  \((\mathtt{extendScalars}\,\psi).\mathtt{Monoidal}\): for a ring map
  \(\psi : R \to S\) it is the canonical isomorphism \(R \otimes_R S \cong S\)
  exhibiting that extension of scalars carries the base ring \(R\) (the
  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) unit over \(R\)) to \(S\) (the unit over \(S\)). Assembling
  these sectionwise unit comparisons across the presheaf and sheafifying descends to
  the asserted isomorphism \(f^*\mathcal{O}_X \cong \mathcal{O}_Y\).

  A morphism \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit} : f^*\mathcal{O}_X \to
  \mathcal{O}_Y\) already exists in Mathlib (it is consumed in
  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial.pullback}\), where it is shown to be an isomorphism for
  an open immersion by a finality argument on the comma category of the open
  cover). For a \emph{general} morphism \(f\) that finality argument is unavailable,
  so whether \(\mathtt{pullbackObjUnitToUnit}\) is itself an isomorphism in general
  must be checked separately; if it is not directly available as an iso, the iso is
  obtained instead from the \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\) unit comparison
  \(\varepsilon\) above, which is an isomorphism for every ring map and hence
  descends to one for every \(f\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Pullback preserves $\otimes$-invertibility for a general scheme morphism]
  \label{lem:isinvertible_pullback}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.IsInvertible.pullback}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible, lem:pullback_tensor_iso,
        lem:pullback_unit_iso}
  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let
  \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). If \(M\) is \(\otimes\)-invertible
  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), then so is its pullback \(f^*M\):
  \[
    \mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)
      \;\Longrightarrow\;
    \mathtt{IsInvertible}\bigl((\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f).\mathrm{obj}\,M\bigr).
  \]
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
  % \ref{lemma-pullback-invertible} (the pullback of an invertible module is
  % invertible) (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4142-4147).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "\begin{lemma}
  % \label{lemma-pullback-invertible}
  % Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be a
  % morphism of ringed spaces. The pullback $f^*\mathcal{L}$ of an
  % invertible $\mathcal{O}_Y$-module is invertible.
  % \end{lemma}"
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
  \texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}: the pullback \(f^*\mathcal{L}\) of an
  invertible \(\mathcal{O}\)-module along a morphism of ringed spaces is
  invertible.}
\end{lemma}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4149-4157).
% "\begin{proof}
% By Lemma \ref{lemma-invertible}
% there exists an $\mathcal{O}_Y$-module $\mathcal{N}$ such that
% $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_Y} \mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_Y$.
% Pulling back we get
% $f^*\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} f^*\mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_X$
% by Lemma \ref{lemma-tensor-product-pullback}.
% Thus $f^*\mathcal{L}$ is invertible by Lemma \ref{lemma-invertible}.
% \end{proof}"
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:pullback_tensor_iso, lem:pullback_unit_iso}
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Lemma \texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}.}
  This is the Stacks proof in the project's notation. By
  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) there is a
  witness \(N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) together with an isomorphism
  \(e : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\). Take the pullback witness
  \((\mathtt{pullback}\,f).\mathrm{obj}\,N = f^*N\). The required isomorphism
  exhibiting \(f^*M\) as \(\otimes\)-invertible is the composite
  \[
    (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N)
      \;\xrightarrow[\sim]{\;\mathtt{pullbackTensorIso}^{-1}\;}\;
    f^*(M \otimes_X N)
      \;\xrightarrow[\sim]{\;f^*e\;}\;
    f^*\mathcal{O}_X
      \;\xrightarrow[\sim]{\;\mathtt{pullbackUnitIso}\;}\;
    \mathcal{O}_Y,
  \]
  i.e.\ \(\mathtt{pullbackTensorIso}.\mathtt{symm} \;\ggg\;
  (\mathtt{pullback}\,f).\mathtt{mapIso}\,e \;\ggg\; \mathtt{pullbackUnitIso}\),
  using \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso} to commute the pullback past the tensor and
  \cref{lem:pullback_unit_iso} to identify \(f^*\mathcal{O}_X\) with
  \(\mathcal{O}_Y\). Hence \(f^*N\) is a tensor inverse of \(f^*M\), so
  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(f^*M)\) holds.
\end{proof}

\section{The invertibility-carrier Picard group}
\label{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}
